Collect & Analyze Finance Data from LSEG with Python¶

Alexandre Garel, Finance Professor, Audencia, 2025–2026

1. Global Configuration¶

Before running any of the examples below, run the next cell once. It performs three housekeeping tasks that every subsequent cell depends on.

  1. BASE_FOLDER — We define a single folder path where all CSV files produced in this notebook will be saved. You should change this path to a folder that exists on your own computer.
  2. Pandas display options — By default, pandas (the main data-manipulation library we use) truncates wide tables. We tell it to show all columns and up to 60 rows so that our outputs are easier to read.
  3. Warning suppression — Some libraries print non-critical warning messages. We silence them to keep the notebook output clean. If you ever need to debug an issue, you can comment out the warnings.filterwarnings("ignore") line.
In [4]:
# 'warnings' lets us control Python warning messages.
import warnings  # import statement: makes external code (a module/package) available in this cell
import pandas as pd # to deal with dataframe settings
import os 

# Set the folder where every CSV produced in this notebook will be saved.
# >>> Change this path to a folder that exists on YOUR computer. <<<
BASE_FOLDER = r"C:\Users\alexa\Documents\Research methods 2" 
os.chdir(BASE_FOLDER) #define working folder
    
# Suppress non-critical warnings so the notebook output stays readable.
# Remove or comment out this line when debugging.
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore") 

# Tell pandas to display ALL columns (not truncate wide tables)
# and to show at most 60 rows before summarising.
pd.set_option("display.max_columns", None) 
pd.set_option("display.max_rows", 60)

Running code. Each gray box below is a code cell. Click inside it and press Shift+Enter (or click Run) to execute the code. The result appears under the cell. Run cells from top to bottom when the text says so.

Variables. You store values in variables with =. Once a variable exists, you can use it in later lines or cells.

Example:

In [5]:
# Variables and simple operations
x = 10
y = 3
print("x =", x, ", y =", y)
print("x + y =", x + y)
print("x * y =", x * y)

x = 10 , y = 3
x + y = 13
x * y = 30

pandas: Series¶

A Series is a single column of data with a name and an index (row labels). You can do math, filter, and use .dropna() on it. We can also turn values to numerical values using .to_numeric().

Below we introduce arrays and show how to use the pandas package to turn string values to numerical values and drop missing observations.

In [6]:
# List of strings (some are numbers, one is not)
strings = ["1.5", "2.7", "missing", "4.0"]
print("Strings:", strings)

Strings: ['1.5', '2.7', 'missing', '4.0']
In [7]:
import pandas as pd
# Turn to numeric: invalid text becomes NaN
numeric = pd.to_numeric(pd.Series(strings), errors="coerce")
print("Numeric (with NaN):", numeric.tolist())

Numeric (with NaN): [1.5, 2.7, nan, 4.0]
In [8]:
# Drop missing values
clean = numeric.dropna()
print("After dropna():", clean.tolist())

After dropna(): [1.5, 2.7, 4.0]

pandas: DataFrames¶

What is a DataFrame? A DataFrame is a table: several columns (each a Series) sharing the same index. Rows = observations, columns = variables. Most datasets (CSV, Excel, etc.) are loaded as DataFrames.

How to handle them:

  • Create: pd.DataFrame({"col1": [1,2,3], "col2": ["a","b","c"]})
  • Columns: df["col_name"] or df.col_name gives a Series.
  • Rows: df.loc[0] (by label), df.iloc[0] (by position).
  • Rename columns: df.rename(columns={"old_name": "new_name"})
  • Subset rows: df[df["col"] > 0]
In [9]:
import pandas as pd

# Create a DataFrame
df = pd.DataFrame({"score": [85, 90, 78], "name": ["Alice", "Bob", "Charlie"]})
print("DataFrame:")
print(df)

# Columns: each column is a Series
print("\nColumn 'score' (Series):")
print(df["score"])

# Rows: by label (loc) or position (iloc)
print("\nFirst row (loc):", df.loc[0].tolist())
print("First row (iloc):", df.iloc[0].tolist())

# Rename columns
df_renamed = df.rename(columns={"score": "grade", "name": "student"})
print("\nAfter rename:")
print(df_renamed)

# Subset: keep only rows where grade > 82
print("\nRows where grade > 82:")
print(df_renamed[df_renamed["grade"] > 82])

DataFrame:
   score     name
0     85    Alice
1     90      Bob
2     78  Charlie

Column 'score' (Series):
0    85
1    90
2    78
Name: score, dtype: int64

First row (loc): [85, 'Alice']
First row (iloc): [85, 'Alice']

After rename:
   grade  student
0     85    Alice
1     90      Bob
2     78  Charlie

Rows where grade > 82:
   grade student
0     85   Alice
1     90     Bob

Other core DataFrame functions¶

Inspecting data

  • df.head(n) — first n rows (default 5). Use df.tail(n) for the last n rows.
  • df.shape — number of rows and columns, e.g. (100, 5).
  • df.columns — list of column names.
  • df.dtypes — type of each column (int, float, object, etc.).

Combining tables: merge

  • pd.merge(left_df, right_df, on="col") — join two DataFrames on a common column (like SQL JOIN). Use how="left", "right", "outer", "inner" to choose the type of join.

Other useful operations

  • df.dropna() — drop rows with missing values (NaN).
  • df.fillna(value) — replace missing values with a value.
  • df.sort_values("col") — sort rows by a column (use ascending=False for descending).
  • df.describe() — summary statistics (count, mean, std, min, max, etc.) for numeric columns.
In [10]:
import pandas as pd

# Two small tables
grades = pd.DataFrame({"student_id": [1, 2, 3], "grade": [85, 90, 78]})
names  = pd.DataFrame({"student_id": [1, 2, 3], "name": ["Alice", "Bob", "Charlie"]})

print("Table 1 (grades):")
print(grades)
print("\nTable 2 (names):")
print(names)

# head / tail / shape
print("\nFirst 2 rows (head):")
print(grades.head(2))
print("Shape (rows, cols):", grades.shape)
print("Column names:", grades.columns.tolist())

# merge: join on student_id
merged = pd.merge(grades, names, on="student_id")
print("\nMerged (join on 'student_id'):")
print(merged)

# sort and describe
print("\nSorted by grade (descending):")
print(merged.sort_values("grade", ascending=False))
print("\nSummary stats (describe):")
print(merged.describe())

Table 1 (grades):
   student_id  grade
0           1     85
1           2     90
2           3     78

Table 2 (names):
   student_id     name
0           1    Alice
1           2      Bob
2           3  Charlie

First 2 rows (head):
   student_id  grade
0           1     85
1           2     90
Shape (rows, cols): (3, 2)
Column names: ['student_id', 'grade']

Merged (join on 'student_id'):
   student_id  grade     name
0           1     85    Alice
1           2     90      Bob
2           3     78  Charlie

Sorted by grade (descending):
   student_id  grade     name
1           2     90      Bob
0           1     85    Alice
2           3     78  Charlie

Summary stats (describe):
       student_id      grade
count         3.0   3.000000
mean          2.0  84.333333
std           1.0   6.027714
min           1.0  78.000000
25%           1.5  81.500000
50%           2.0  85.000000
75%           2.5  87.500000
max           3.0  90.000000

2. Collecting Data from Refinitiv / LSEG with Python¶

In this section we learn how to connect to the Refinitiv / LSEG financial data platform and download market, accounting, ESG, and news data directly into Python. Every subsection follows the same pattern: open a connection, request specific data by specifying an instrument and a set of fields, inspect the result, save it as a CSV file, and close the connection.

2.1. What is a Python package?¶

A Python package is a collection of pre-written code that provides specific functionality. Instead of writing everything from scratch, we install a package and call the functions it provides.

Packages are installed with the pip tool. When running pip from inside a Jupyter Notebook cell, prefix the command with ! (for example !pip install refinitiv-data).

refinitiv-data is the official Python SDK (Software Development Kit) provided by Refinitiv (now LSEG). It lets us programmatically access:

  • Market data (prices, time series, intraday data).
  • Reference data (instruments, identifiers, metadata).
  • Fundamental and economic data (accounting, ratios).
  • News and analytics.

The package handles authentication, data requests, and responses behind the scenes. The cell below installs it into the current Anaconda environment. If it is already installed, pip will simply report that the requirement is satisfied.

In [11]:
!pip install refinitiv-data

2.2. Reading official documentation¶

Once a package is installed, the next step is to read its official documentation. Official documentation is the most reliable source because it describes the intended usage, explains inputs and outputs, provides examples, and reflects the most up-to-date behaviour of the package.

Key documentation sources for the Refinitiv Data Library:

  • GitHub repository: https://github.com/refinitiv/refinitiv-data
  • Developer portal: https://developers.refinitiv.com/

Alternatively, you can ask an AI assistant (Claude is recommended) to help generate code. AI can draft code snippets, explain unfamiliar functions, help debug errors, and suggest efficient approaches. However, AI-generated code must always be reviewed to ensure you understand what it does, can validate the outputs, and can catch any outdated or incorrect API usage.

A robust approach is to constrain the AI by providing the official documentation as input and asking it to answer only based on that material, or by asking it to consult the official online documentation first.

Two practical tools inside Python for self-documentation are:

  • help() — Displays the signature and parameters of a function. Example: help(rd.get_data).
  • search.metadata.Definition(view=...) — Returns a table of all properties available for a given Search view (for example EQUITY_QUOTES, GOV_CORP_INSTRUMENTS), indicating which ones are searchable, groupable, and filterable.

Whenever you design a new query, it is good practice to first read the relevant section of the LSEG documentation and then inspect the metadata and help() output in Python so that your filters and field lists are based on what the platform actually supports.

2.3. Exploring available functions and fields¶

Before writing a data query, you may want to know what functions are available in the rd module and how to use them. Python provides two built-in helpers for this.

dir() — Lists every function and attribute available in a module. Running dir(rd) prints the names of everything you can call on the rd object.

help() — Shows the full documentation of a specific function, including its parameters, their types, default values, and a short description. For example, help(rd.get_history) explains what arguments get_history accepts.

The cell below demonstrates both.

In [12]:
# Import Refinitiv Package
import refinitiv.data as rd 
# List all available functions and attributes in the rd module.
print(dir(rd))  

['OpenState', 'PricingStream', 'close_session', 'content', 'dates_and_calendars', 'delivery', 'discovery', 'eikon', 'errors', 'get_config', 'get_data', 'get_history', 'load_config', 'news', 'open_pricing_stream', 'open_session', 'session', 'usage_collection']

Here we have used the command print, print() sends information from your program to the standard output, usually the screen (console/terminal). It is important because it’s the simplest way to see what your code is doing. It connects the invisible internal world of the program (variables, calculations, logic) with what you can actually see and understand as a human.

In [13]:
name = "Alex"  # variable assignment: store a value in 'name' so we can reuse it later
age = 21  # variable assignment: store a value in 'age' so we can reuse it later
print("Name:", name)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
print("Age:", age)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

Name: Alex
Age: 21
In [14]:
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
# Get help on the function get_data
help(rd.get_data) 

Help on function get_data in module refinitiv.data._access_layer.get_data_func:

get_data(universe: Union[str, Iterable[str]], fields: Union[str, Iterable[str], NoneType] = None, parameters: Union[str, dict, NoneType] = None, use_field_names_in_headers: bool = None) -> pandas.core.frame.DataFrame
    Retrieves pricing snapshots, as well as Fundamental and Reference data.

    Parameters
    ----------
    universe: str | list
        Instruments to request
    fields: str | list, optional
        Fields to request
    parameters: str | dict, optional
        Single key=value global parameter or dictionary of global parameters to request
    use_field_names_in_headers: bool, default False
        If True - returns field name as column headers for data instead of title

    Returns
    -------
    pandas.DataFrame

    Examples
    --------
    >>> get_data(universe=['IBM.N', 'VOD.L'], fields=['BID', 'ASK'])
    >>> get_data(
    ...     universe=['GOOG.O', 'AAPL.O'],
    ...     fields=['TR.EV','TR.EVToSales'],
    ...     parameters = {'SDate': '0CY', 'Curn': 'CAD'}
    ...)
In [15]:
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
# Get help on function get_history
help(rd.get_history) 

Help on function get_history in module refinitiv.data._access_layer.get_history_func:

get_history(universe: Union[str, Iterable[str]], fields: Union[str, Iterable[str], NoneType] = None, interval: Optional[str] = None, start: 'OptDateTime' = None, end: 'OptDateTime' = None, adjustments: Optional[str] = None, count: Optional[int] = None, use_field_names_in_headers: bool = None, parameters: Union[str, dict, NoneType] = None) -> pandas.core.frame.DataFrame
    Retrieves the pricing history, as well as Fundamental and Reference data history.

    Parameters
    ----------
    universe: str | list
        Instruments to request
    fields: str | list, optional
        Fields to request
    interval: str, optional
        Date interval. Supported intervals are:
        tick, tas, taq, minute, 1min, 5min, 10min, 30min, 60min, hourly, 1h, daily,
        1d, 1D, 7D, 7d, weekly, 1W, monthly, 1M, quarterly, 3M, 6M, yearly, 1Y
    start: str or date or datetime or timedelta, optional
        The start date and timestamp of the requested history
    end: str or date or datetime or timedelta, optional
        The end date and timestamp of the requested history
    adjustments : str, optional
        Tells the system whether to apply or not apply CORAX (Corporate Actions)
        events or exchange/manual corrections or price and volume adjustment
        according to trade/quote qualifier summarization actions to historical time
        series data. Possible values are:
        exchangeCorrection, manualCorrection, CCH, CRE, RTS, RPO, unadjusted,
        qualifiers
    count : int, optional
        The maximum number of data points returned. Values range: 1 - 10000.
        Applies only to pricing fields.
    use_field_names_in_headers : bool, default False
        If True - returns field name as column headers for data instead of title
    parameters: str | dict, optional
        Single global parameter key=value or dictionary
        of global parameters to request.
        Applies only to TR fields.

    Returns
    -------
    pandas.DataFrame

    Examples
    --------
    >>> get_history(universe="GOOG.O")
    >>> get_history(universe="GOOG.O", fields="tr.Revenue", interval="1Y")
    >>> get_history(
    ...     universe="GOOG.O",
    ...     fields=["BID", "ASK", "tr.Revenue"],
    ...     interval="1Y",
    ...     start="2015-01-01",
    ...     end="2020-10-01",
    ... )

2.4. A first data extraction¶

In this subsection we run a first simple extraction to illustrate the basic workflow.

Step-by-step logic:

  1. Import the package and give it the short alias rd so we can write rd.get_history() instead of refinitiv.data.get_history().
  2. Open a session with rd.open_session(). This authenticates you and establishes a connection to Refinitiv services. It automatically detects a running Refinitiv Workspace application.
  3. Request data with rd.get_history(). We provide:
    • universe — the instrument we want data for, identified by its RIC (Reuters Instrument Code). For example, "AAPL.O" is Apple Inc. on NASDAQ.
    • fields — the specific data points to retrieve, here "BID" (the price a buyer is willing to pay) and "ASK" (the price a seller is willing to accept).
    • start / end — the date range for the historical data.
  4. Save the result as a CSV file so we can reuse it later without re-downloading.
  5. Close the session with rd.close_session() to release the connection.

The function returns a DataFrame — the core pandas object, which you can think of as a spreadsheet stored in Python's memory.

In [16]:
# --- Imports needed for this cell (so it can run independently) ---
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)

# Open the session
rd.open_session()

# Request historical BID and ASK prices for Apple (AAPL.O) over 2024–2025.
# 'universe' = the instrument; 'fields' = the data points; 'start'/'end' = date range.
df = rd.get_history(  
    universe="AAPL.O",  # Apple's RIC (identifier in Refinitiv)
    fields=["BID", "ASK"],  # varaible we want to collect
    interval="daily", # interval
    start="2024-01-01",  # start date
    end="2025-12-31"  # end date
) 

# Close the session to release the connection.
rd.close_session() 

print(df)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# Save the data as a CSV file in the base folder defined in Section 1.
df.to_csv("aapl.csv")  # to_csv() writes a DataFrame to a CSV file on dis

AAPL.O         BID     ASK
Date                      
2024-01-02  185.52  185.53
2024-01-03  184.24  184.25
2024-01-04  181.91  181.92
2024-01-05  181.17  181.19
2024-01-08  185.54  185.55
...            ...     ...
2025-12-24  273.82  273.88
2025-12-26  273.25  273.45
2025-12-29  273.74  273.76
2025-12-30  272.98  273.09
2025-12-31  271.84  271.94

[502 rows x 2 columns]

What fields can I retrieve for a given instrument?

For a complete catalogue of all available data items and their exact code names (called TR mnemonics), use two desktop tools inside Refinitiv Workspace:

  • Data Item Browser (DIB) — type DIB in the Workspace search bar. It lets you browse all data items and see their exact mnemonics.
  • CodeCreator (CODECR) — type CODECR in the Workspace search bar. It lets you select data items and automatically generates example Python code that you can copy-paste directly into your notebook.

Application. Now it is your turn to practice. Find the RIC for Microsoft, read the help for get_history() to understand the parameters it can take and look at the variables we can retrieve with it. You must retrieve for Microsoft the monthly time series of volume over 2023. Try to do it yourself. A solution is provided below (click to unhide it).

In [17]:
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)

# Open a session.
rd.open_session()  # rd.open_session() starts an authenticated connection to Refinitiv/LSEG (needed before queries)

# collect data,
# traded volume: ACVOL_UNS
df = rd.get_history(  
universe="MSFT.O",  
fields=["ACVOL_UNS"], 
interval="1M",  #monthly
start="2023-01-01",  # start date
end="2023-12-31")  # end date

# Close the session.
rd.close_session() 

# show dataframe
print(df)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

# save
df.to_csv("volume_MSFT.csv")

MSFT.O      ACVOL_UNS
Date                 
2023-01-31  666168068
2023-02-28  615540778
2023-03-31  747751976
2023-04-30  551520127
2023-05-31  600856941
2023-06-30  547748450
2023-07-31  666956355
2023-08-31  479669427
2023-09-30  416768824
2023-10-31  540963749
2023-11-30  564165364
2023-12-31  522081429

2.5. Finding the right RIC identifier and collecting returns¶

Before downloading data for a specific stock or index, you need its RIC (Reuters Instrument Code) — a short alphanumeric identifier that uniquely identifies an instrument on Refinitiv. For example, "AAPL.O" is Apple on NASDAQ and ".FCHI" is the CAC 40 price index.

You can look up a RIC in two ways:

  1. Programmatically — Use rd.discovery.search(), which queries the Refinitiv search engine. You provide a free-text query (e.g. "Amazon"), a view (e.g. EQUITY_QUOTES for stocks, INDEX_QUOTES for indices), and a top parameter that limits the number of results.
  2. Interactively — Type the company name directly in the Refinitiv Workspace search bar and note the RIC shown next to the correct instrument.

The cell below shows the help() output for rd.discovery.search so you can see all its parameters, then demonstrates a search for Amazon.

In [18]:
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
# --- Connect to Refinitiv/LSEG (requires an account + app key setup) ---
rd.open_session()
# Show the full documentation for the discovery.search function.
help(rd.discovery.search) 

Help on function search in module refinitiv.data.discovery._search:

search(query: Optional[str] = None, view: Union[refinitiv.data.content.search._views.Views, str] = <Views.SEARCH_ALL: 'SearchAll'>, filter: Optional[str] = None, order_by: Optional[str] = None, boost: Optional[str] = None, select: Optional[str] = None, top: int = 10, skip: int = 0, group_by: Optional[str] = None, group_count: int = 3, features: Optional[str] = None, scope: Optional[str] = None, terms: Optional[str] = None) -> 'pd.DataFrame'
    This class describe parameters to retrieve data for search.

    Parameters
    ----------
    query: str, optional
        Keyword argument for view

    view: Views or str, optional
        The view for searching see at Views enum.
        Default: Views.SEARCH_ALL

    filter: str, optional
        Where query is for unstructured end-user-oriented restriction, filter is for
        structured programmatic restriction.

    order_by: str, optional
        Defines the order in which matching documents should be returned.

    boost: str, optional
        This argument supports exactly the same predicate expression syntax as filter,
        but where filter restricts which documents are matched at all,
        boost just applies a large scoring boost to documents it matches,
        which will almost always guarantee that they appear at the top of the results.

    select: str, optional
        A comma-separated list of the properties of a document to be returned in the response.

    top: int, optional
        the maximum number of documents to retrieve. Must be non-negative.
        default: 10

    skip: int, optional
        The number of documents to skip in the sorted result set before returning the
        next top.

    group_by: str, optional
        If specified, this must name a single Groupable property.
        returned documents are grouped into buckets based on their value for this
        property.

    group_count: str, optional
        When supplied in combination with group_by, sets the maximum number of documents
        to be returned per bucket.
        default: 3

    Examples
    --------
    >>> from refinitiv.data as rd
    >>> df = rd.discovery.search(query="cfo", view=rd.discovery.Views.PEOPLE)

The cell below lists all available Search views. Each view gives access to a different category of instrument or dataset (equities, indices, bonds, deals, investors, etc.).

In [19]:
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
rd.open_session()
# List all available Search views (e.g. EQUITY_QUOTES, INDEX_QUOTES, etc.).
# Each view gives access to a different type of instrument or dataset.
views = [v for v in dir(rd.discovery.Views) if not v.startswith("__")]  # variable assignment: store a value in 'views' so we can reuse it later
for i, v in enumerate(views, start=1):  # for-loop: repeat code for each element in an iterable
    print(f"{i:2d}. {v}")  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
rd.close_session()

The cell below searches for Amazon within the EQUITY_QUOTES view and returns the top 10 matches. Inspect the RIC column to find the correct identifier.

In [20]:
import refinitiv.data as rd
rd.open_session() #open session
# Search for "Amazon" in the EQUITY_QUOTES view (stocks only).
# 'top=10' limits the results to the 10 best matches.
results = rd.discovery.search(  
    query="Amazon",  
    view=rd.discovery.Views.EQUITY_QUOTES,  # listed company view
    top=10
) 
rd.close_session() #close session
print(results) # visualize dataframe

1   QUOTExEQUITY  Eurex Amazon Equity Total Return Future Chain ...

3   QUOTExEQUITY  Intercontinental Exchange Amazon Flex Equity F...   
4   QUOTExEQUITY  Johannesburg Stock Exchange Amazon Com Interna...   
5   QUOTExEQUITY  Euronext Amsterdam Amazon.com Dividend Future ...   
6   QUOTExEQUITY  Johannesburg Stock Exchange Amazon Com IDX Equ...   
7   QUOTExEQUITY  Eurex Amazon Equity Total Return Future Contin...   
8   QUOTExEQUITY  Eurex Amazon Equity Total Return Future Contin...   
9   QUOTExEQUITY  Euronext Amsterdam Amazon com Single Stock Div...   

        PermID         PI        RIC  
0  55839008192     746733     AMZN.O  
1  22161353450  991973454    0#TAMZ:  
2  22090423093  876385463    0#AMZX:  
3  22145658562  971973710   0#8A7FL:

6  21481187383   79461287   0#AMZIG:  
7  22161489420  997805045     TAMZc1  
8  22161489429  997805046     TAMZc2  
9  21612960409  259721843   AMZNDFc1

Once we have the correct RIC (for example "AMZN.O" for Amazon on NASDAQ), we can retrieve a time series of monthly total returns. A total return includes both the change in the stock price and any dividends paid during the period, which gives a more complete picture of the return to shareholders than price returns alone.

The field TR.TotalReturn1Mo provides the one-month total return. We set interval="monthly" so that we get one observation per month.

In [21]:
import refinitiv.data as rd 
rd.open_session()
# Download monthly total returns (including dividends) for Amazon (AMZN.O).
df = rd.get_history( 
    universe=["AMZN.O"],  
    fields=["TR.TotalReturn1Mo"],   # one-month total return
    interval="monthly",             # one observation per calendar month
    start="2004-01-01",  # start date
    end="2024-12-31"  # end date
) 
rd.close_session() 
print(df)
df.to_csv("AMZN_TR_monthly.csv")

AMZN.O      1 Month Total Return

2004-02-27            -12.616822
2004-03-31              0.627761
2004-04-30             -0.456621
2004-05-28             11.238532
...                          ...
2024-08-30             -4.535244
2024-09-30              4.386555
2024-10-31              0.037568
2024-11-29              7.865926
2024-12-31              5.531772

[252 rows x 1 columns]

Application. Now it is your turn to practice. Find the RIC for TESLA using the approach above and then retrieve daily total return over 2024. A solution is provided below (click to unhide it).

In [22]:
import refinitiv.data as rd
rd.open_session() #open session
# Search for "Amazon" in the EQUITY_QUOTES view (stocks only).
# 'top=10' limits the results to the 10 best matches.
results = rd.discovery.search(  
    query="Tesla",  
    view=rd.discovery.Views.EQUITY_QUOTES,  # listed company view
    top=10
) 
rd.close_session() #close session
print(results) # visualize dataframe

In [23]:
import refinitiv.data as rd
rd.open_session() #open session
# Download daily total returns (including dividends) for TESLA (TSLA.O).
df = rd.get_history(
    universe=["TSLA.O"], 
    fields=["TR.TotalReturn1D"],   
    interval="daily",             # one observation per calendar day
    start="2024-01-01",  # start date
    end="2024-12-31"  # end date
) 
rd.close_session() # close session
print(df)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

We now search for the RIC of the CAC 40 index. The CAC 40 is the main French stock market index, composed of the 40 largest companies listed on Euronext Paris. We search in the INDEX_QUOTES view.

In [24]:
import refinitiv.data as rd
rd.open_session()
# Search for "CAC40" in the INDEX_QUOTES view (indices only).
results = rd.discovery.search(
    query="CAC40", #the index we are looking for
    view=rd.discovery.Views.INDEX_QUOTES, # the view universe where we want to search (INDEXES)
    top=10  
) 
rd.close_session() #close session
print(results)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)

  BusinessEntity                                      DocumentTitle  \
0    QUOTExINDEX         CAC 40 Index, Equity Index, Euronext Paris   
1    QUOTExINDEX  CAC 40 Index, Equity Index, Chain, Euronext Paris   
2    QUOTExINDEX  CAC 40 Gross Total Return Index, Equity Index,...   
3    QUOTExINDEX  CAC 40 Net Total Return, Equity Index, Euronex...   
4    QUOTExINDEX     CAC 40 ESG Index, Equity Index, Euronext Paris   
5    QUOTExINDEX  CAC 40 Equal Weight GR Index, Equity Index, Eu...   
6    QUOTExINDEX  CAC 40 X2 Short Index, Equity Index, Euronext ...   
7    QUOTExINDEX   CAC 40 Short Index, Equity Index, Euronext Paris   
8    QUOTExINDEX  CAC 40 Synthetic Dividend Index, Equity Index,...   
9    QUOTExINDEX  CAC40 Eq Weight Index, Equity Index, Euronext ...   

         PermID         PI      RIC  
0   71966969118    1033356    .FCHI  
1          <NA>   12308819  0#.FCHI  
2   69578169762   49801228   .PX1GR  
3   71037077632   49801208   .PX1NR  
4   72409723179  502602020   .CESGP  
5   55869215877   79709281   .CACEG  
6   72687075938   56819320   .CAC2S  
7   55856795777   42171026   .CACSH  
8  130604843025  258995762    .C4SD  
9   55856795774   60996262   .CACEW

For indices, total return fields often do not work on the standard price-only RIC (like .FCHI for the CAC 40) because that RIC tracks price movements only and ignores dividends. The solution is to use the gross total return version of the index, which has a different RIC. For the CAC 40, the gross total return index is ".PX1GR".

From the index level we compute monthly returns using the .pct_change() method, which calculates the percentage change from one row to the next: return_t = (level_t / level_{t-1}) - 1.

In [25]:
import pandas as pd  
import refinitiv.data as rd  
rd.open_session()
# Request the monthly level of the CAC 40 gross total return index.
# - universe=".PX1GR" tells Refinitiv which instrument we want (the CAC 40 gross total return index RIC).
# - fields=["TRDPRC_1"] asks for the last price (index level) at each date.
# - interval="monthly" asks for monthly observations instead of daily data.
# - start / end specify the date range of the history.
df = rd.get_history( 
    universe=".PX1GR",  
    fields=["TRDPRC_1"],  
    interval="monthly",  
    start="2005-01-01",  
    end="2024-12-31",) 
rd.close_session()
# print dataframe
print(df)
# Compute monthly returns as the percentage change between consecutive index levels.
# df.pct_change() computes (value_t / value_{t-1}) - 1 for each row.
# The first row has no previous observation, so pct_change() returns NaN there;
# .dropna() removes that first row with the missing value.
returns = df.pct_change().dropna()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'returns' so we can reuse it later
print(returns)
#save
returns.to_csv("cac40_gross_returns.csv")

.PX1GR      TRDPRC_1
Date                
2005-01-31   6318.85
2005-02-28   6502.05
2005-03-31   6567.64
2005-04-30   6329.85
2005-05-31    6774.6
...              ...
2024-08-31  23742.54
2024-09-30  23783.25
2024-10-31  22901.87
2024-11-30  22550.65
2024-12-31  23033.78

[240 rows x 1 columns]
.PX1GR      TRDPRC_1
Date                
2005-02-28  0.028993
2005-03-31  0.010088
2005-04-30 -0.036206
2005-05-31  0.070262
2005-06-30  0.031456
...              ...
2024-08-31  0.013206
2024-09-30  0.001715
2024-10-31 -0.037059
2024-11-30 -0.015336
2024-12-31  0.021424

[239 rows x 1 columns]

Application. Now it is your turn to practice. Find the RIC for gross return of the EUROSTOXX 600 using the approach above and then retrieve yearly gross total returns over the last 20 years. A solution is provided below (click to unhide it).

In [26]:
import refinitiv.data as rd 
rd.open_session()
# Search for "STOXX 600" in the INDEX_QUOTES view (indices only).
results = rd.discovery.search(  
    query="STOXX 600", 
    view=rd.discovery.Views.INDEX_QUOTES,  
    top=10) 
rd.close_session() 
print(results)

In [27]:
# returns for this index
import refinitiv.data as rd
rd.open_session()
# Download the monthly level of the STOXX 600 gross total return index.
# TRDPRC_1 is the "last price" field commonly used for index levels.
df = rd.get_history(  
    universe=".STOXX",  
    fields=["TRDPRC_1"],  
    interval="1Y",  # yearly interval
    start="2005-01-01",  #  start date
    end="2025-01-01"  # end date
) 
rd.close_session() 
# .pct_change() returns NaN for the first row (no previous level), so we drop it.
returns = df.pct_change().dropna() 
print(returns)
# Save search results to CSV.
returns.to_csv("stox600_gross_returns.csv")

.STOXX      TRDPRC_1
Date

2007-12-31 -0.001697
2008-12-31 -0.456011

2011-12-31 -0.113375
2012-12-31  0.143698
2013-12-31  0.173699
2014-12-31  0.043502
2015-12-31  0.067934
2016-12-31 -0.012001
2017-12-31  0.076808
2018-12-31 -0.132407
2019-12-31  0.231571
2020-12-31 -0.040424
2021-12-31  0.222464
2022-12-31 -0.128967
2023-12-31  0.127327
2024-12-31  0.059772
2025-12-31  0.166601

2.6. Collecting accounting information for one stock¶

Using get_data(), we can collect accounting information for a given stock (over a given period).

In [28]:
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
help(rd.get_data) 

Help on function get_data in module refinitiv.data._access_layer.get_data_func:

get_data(universe: Union[str, Iterable[str]], fields: Union[str, Iterable[str], NoneType] = None, parameters: Union[str, dict, NoneType] = None, use_field_names_in_headers: bool = None) -> pandas.core.frame.DataFrame
    Retrieves pricing snapshots, as well as Fundamental and Reference data.

    Parameters
    ----------
    universe: str | list
        Instruments to request
    fields: str | list, optional
        Fields to request
    parameters: str | dict, optional
        Single key=value global parameter or dictionary of global parameters to request
    use_field_names_in_headers: bool, default False
        If True - returns field name as column headers for data instead of title

    Returns
    -------
    pandas.DataFrame

    Examples
    --------
    >>> get_data(universe=['IBM.N', 'VOD.L'], fields=['BID', 'ASK'])
    >>> get_data(
    ...     universe=['GOOG.O', 'AAPL.O'],
    ...     fields=['TR.EV','TR.EVToSales'],
    ...     parameters = {'SDate': '0CY', 'Curn': 'CAD'}
    ...)

As before, we look up for the right variable name in Refinitiv using either:

  • Data Item Browser (DIB) — type DIB in the Workspace search bar.
  • CodeCreator (CODECR) — type CODECR in the Workspace search bar.

Doing so we find that for a stock such as TESLA, the right variable name for net income is TR.NetIncome. We retrieve it over the last ten years.

In [29]:
#use get_data():
import refinitiv.data as rd
rd.open_session()
# Define identifier
ric = "TSLA.O"  # TESAL identifier
# Define the sample period and frequency.
# SDate / EDate = start / end; Frq = "FY" means one observation per fiscal year.
params = {  # variable assignment: store a value in 'params' so we can reuse it later
    "SDate": "2014-01-01", 
    "EDate": "2024-12-31", 
    "Frq":   "FY" 
} 
# Define the raw accounting fields to download.
# Field mnemonics must always be verified in the Data Item Browser (DIB).
# We define start date, end ate, and frequency (fiscal years)
fields = ["TR.NetIncome", "TR.NetIncome.Date"]  # variable assignment: store a value in 'fields' so we can reuse it later
# Download the panel using rd.get_data().
tesla_data = rd.get_data(  
    universe=ric, 
    fields=fields,
    parameters={ "SDate": "2014-01-01", "EDate": "2024-12-31", "Frq": "FY"}) 
# show data
print(tesla_data) 
# save data
tesla_data.to_csv("tesla_net_income.csv")

   Instrument  Net Income Incl Extra Before Distributions       Date
0      TSLA.O                                   -74014000 2013-12-31
1      TSLA.O                                  -294040000 2014-12-31
2      TSLA.O                                  -888663000 2015-12-31
3      TSLA.O                                  -674914000 2016-12-31
4      TSLA.O                                 -1961400000 2017-12-31
5      TSLA.O                                  -976091000 2018-12-31
6      TSLA.O                                  -870000000 2019-12-31
7      TSLA.O                                   690000000 2020-12-31
8      TSLA.O                                  5524000000 2021-12-31
9      TSLA.O                                 12583000000 2022-12-31
10     TSLA.O                                 14999000000 2023-12-31

Application. Now it is your turn to practice. Collect the capital expenditures of the company Dassault over the last five years. First, find the RIC for Dassault, then the variable name for the capital expenditures, finally collect. A solution is provided below (click to unhide it).

In [30]:
import refinitiv.data as rd
rd.open_session()
# Download the panel using rd.get_data().
dassault_data = rd.get_data(
    universe="DAST.PA", 
    fields=["TR.CapitalExpenditures", "TR.CapitalExpenditures.Date"],  
    parameters= { "SDate": "-5Y",  "EDate": "0",  "Frq":   "FY"})
# show data
print(dassault_data)
# save data
dassault_data.to_csv("dassault_capex.csv")

  Instrument  Capital Expenditures, Cumulative       Date
0    DAST.PA                        -103700000 2021-12-31
1    DAST.PA                        -132300000 2022-12-31
2    DAST.PA                        -145300000 2023-12-31
3    DAST.PA                        -193400000 2024-12-31
4    DAST.PA                        -160500000 2025-12-31

2.7. Collecting accounting information for all CAC 40 constituents¶

We now build a panel of accounting variables for all the constituents of the CAC 40 over the last 20 years. A panel (also called longitudinal data) is a dataset where we observe the same set of companies across multiple time periods — here one row per company per fiscal year.

The steps are:

  1. Identify the CAC 40 constituents. We use the index chain RIC 0#.FCHI. We already know that the RIC of the index CAC40 is .FCHI, the 0# prefix is Refinitiv's Chain RIC notation. It tells the system to expand the index into its individual constituents rather than returning the index itself.
  2. Set the time span and frequency. We request 20 years of annual (fiscal-year) data using the parameters argument: SDate (start date), EDate (end date), and Frq (frequency, "FY" for fiscal year).
  3. Request fundamental fields. We use rd.get_data() — designed for fundamental / reference data — rather than rd.get_history() (which is for time-series / market data). The fields requested are the raw accounting items we need to construct standard financial ratios.
  4. Construct clean variables in Python. We rename columns to readable names and compute standard ratios: ROA (Return on Assets), leverage (Debt / Assets), market-to-book, capex intensity, R&D intensity, and log of total assets (a proxy for firm size).
  5. Save the panel as a CSV file so that later sections can reload it without re-downloading.

The cell below first retrieves the current CAC 40 constituent list.

In [31]:
import refinitiv.data as rd
rd.open_session()
# Retrieve the current CAC 40 constituents from the index chain RIC.
# '0#.FCHI' is the chain RIC that lists every member of the CAC 40.
cac_chain = "0#.FCHI"
constituents = rd.get_data(
    universe=cac_chain,
    fields=["TR.RIC", "TR.CommonName"]) 
rd.close_session()
# Print to check list
print(constituents) 
# Save the constituent list to CSV.
constituents.to_csv("cac40_constituents.csv") 

   Instrument       RIC                                Company Common Name

The next cell downloads the 20-year accounting panel and constructs the derived variables. This cell depends on the constituents DataFrame created just above — they must be run in order.

Fields requested (raw accounting items):

Field mnemonic Description Variable name after rename
TR.NetIncome Net income net_income
TR.CompanyMarketCap Market capitalisation market_cap
TR.F.ComEqTot Common equity (book value) equity_value
TR.CapitalExpenditures Capital expenditures capex
TR.TotalAssets Total assets total_assets
TR.RnDExp Research & development expense rd_exp
TR.TotalDebt Total debt total_debt
TR.TotalAssets.date Fiscal period end date period_end

Derived ratios:

Variable Formula Interpretation
ROA Net income / Total assets How efficiently a company uses its assets to generate profit
Leverage Total debt / Total assets How much of the company's assets are financed by debt
Market-to-book Market cap / Equity book value How much investors value the company relative to its accounting value
Capex / Assets Capital expenditures / Total assets Investment intensity
R&D / Assets R&D expense / Total assets Innovation intensity
ln(Total assets) Natural logarithm of total assets Firm size (log-transformed to reduce skewness)
In [32]:
import pandas as pd
import refinitiv.data as rd  
rd.open_session()
# load cac40 consistuents
rics= pd.read_csv("cac40_constituents.csv")
rics= pd.read_csv("cac40_constituents.csv")["RIC"].to_list()
print(rics)
# Download the panel using rd.get_data().
raw_panel = rd.get_data(
    universe=rics,  
    fields=[ "TR.NetIncome", "TR.CompanyMarketCap", "TR.F.ComEqTot", "TR.CapitalExpenditures", "TR.TotalAssets", 
    "TR.RnDExp", "TR.TotalDebt", "TR.TotalAssets.date"],
    parameters={ "SDate": "2005-01-01", "EDate": "2024-12-31", "Frq": "FY"}) 
rd.close_session() 
# show first rows of dataframe
print(raw_panel.head(n=10))
#save
raw_panel.to_csv("raw_panel.csv")

In [33]:
raw_panel.columns
Out[33]:
Index(['Instrument', 'Net Income Incl Extra Before Distributions',
       'Company Market Cap', 'Common Equity - Total',
       'Capital Expenditures, Cumulative', 'Total Assets',
       'R&D Expense - Broker Estimate', 'Total Debt', 'Date'],
      dtype='object')
In [34]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np #package to take log

# load panel data
panel = pd.read_csv("raw_panel.csv")

# ── Compute derived financial ratios ──────────────────────────────────────────
panel["ROA"]            = panel["Net Income Incl Extra Before Distributions"]  / panel["Total Assets"] 
panel["capex_at"]       = panel["Capital Expenditures, Cumulative"]       / panel["Total Assets"] 
panel["ln_at"]          = np.log(panel["Total Assets"]) 
panel["leverage"]       = panel["Total Debt"]  / panel["Total Assets"] 
panel["Market_to_book"] = panel["Company Market Cap"]  / panel["Common Equity - Total"] 
panel["rnd_at"]         = panel["R&D Expense - Broker Estimate"]      / panel["Total Assets"] 

# Quick inspection
print(panel.head(n=10))

# Save the panel as a CSV
panel.to_csv("cac40_fundamentals_2005_2024.csv")

Application. Now it is your turn to practice. For all the constituent of the EURO STOXX 600, retrieve the sales as of 2024. A solution is provided below (click to unhide it).

In [35]:
import refinitiv.data as rd 
rd.open_session()
# Retrieve constituents of STOXX 
constituents = rd.get_data(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'constituents' so we can reuse it later
    universe=["0#.STOXX"],
    fields=["TR.RIC", "TR.CommonName"]  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
) 
print(constituents)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
# Extract the list of RICs from the constituents table.
rics = constituents["RIC"].tolist()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'rics' so we can reuse it later
#keep non void rics
rics = list(filter(None, rics))
# Download the panel using rd.get_data().
raw_panel = rd.get_data(  
    universe=rics, 
    fields=["TR.Revenue", "TR.Revenue.Date"],  
    parameters={"EDate": "2024-12-31"}) 
rd.close_session() 
#Print
print(raw_panel.head()) 
#Save
raw_panel.to_csv("stoxx_sales_2024.csv")

2.8. Downloading monthly total returns for all CAC 40 constituents¶

In Section 2.7 we built a panel of annual accounting variables. For many empirical applications we also need stock returns. In this subsection we download monthly total returns (including dividends) for every CAC 40 stock over the same 2005–2024 window.

We use rd.get_history() (for time-series data) instead of rd.get_data() (which is for snapshot / fundamental data). The field TR.TotalReturn1Mo gives the one-month total return — it includes both price changes and reinvested dividends, which is what we typically want when studying portfolio performance or testing asset-pricing models.

This cell depends on the constituents DataFrame created in Section 2.6. We load the csv file that we have previously created cac40_constituent.csv.

In [37]:
import refinitiv.data as rd
rd.open_session()
# Reload the CAC 40 constituent list from the CSV saved in Section 2.6.
constituents = pd.read_csv("cac40_constituents.csv") 
rics = constituents["RIC"].tolist()
print(rics)
# Download monthly total returns (including dividends).
returns_cac40 = rd.get_history(  
    universe=rics, 
    fields=["TR.TotalReturn1Mo"], 
    interval="monthly",  
    start="2005-01-01", 
    end="2024-12-31")
rd.close_session()
# Print
print(returns_cac40.head())
# Save
returns_cac40.to_csv("cac40_monthly_total_returns_2005_2024.csv")

Application. Now it is your turn to practice. For all the constituent of the S&P 500, collect the time series of monthly returns over the year 2024. A solution is provided below (click to unhide it).

In [38]:
import refinitiv.data as rd
rd.open_session()
constituents = rd.get_data(  # variable assignment: store a value in 'constituents' so we can reuse it later
    universe="0#.SPX",
    fields=["TR.RIC", "TR.CommonName"]  # keyword argument: set the parameter 'fields' for the function call
) 
print(constituents)  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
rics = constituents["RIC"].tolist()  # variable assignment: store a value in 'rics' so we can reuse it later
# Download monthly total returns (including dividends).
returns_sp500 = rd.get_history(  
    universe=rics,
    fields=["TR.TotalReturn1Mo"], 
    interval="monthly",
    start="2024-01-01", 
    end="2024-12-31") 
rd.close_session() 
print(returns_sp500.head())  # print() outputs text/values to the screen (useful for checking results)
# Save to CSV.
returns_sp500.to_csv("sp500_monthly_total_returns_2024.csv")

2.9. Extracting earnings forecasts (EPS) for a single stock¶

Earnings Per Share (EPS) is a company's net profit divided by the number of shares outstanding. Analysts regularly publish forecasts of future EPS — these "consensus" forecasts are important because they reflect the market's expectations about a company's profitability.

Refinitiv provides consensus (summary) EPS forecasts through dedicated fields. Key concepts:

  • TR.EPSMean — The average (consensus) EPS forecast across all analysts covering the stock.
  • TR.EPSActValue — The actual (realised) EPS once the company reports earnings.
  • Period parameter — Controls the forecast horizon: "FY1" = next fiscal year, "FY2" = the year after.
  • Frq parameter — Controls how often the forecast is sampled: "M" = monthly.

The cells below downloads monthly EPS consensus forecasts and actuals for Microsoft (MSFT.O) at two horizons.

In [39]:
import refinitiv.data as rd 
rd.open_session()
# period 'FY1' (next fiscal year)
eps_fy1 = rd.get_data(
    universe="MSFT.O",  
    fields=[ "TR.EPSMean",  "TR.EPSMean.Date",    "TR.EPSActValue" ],  
    parameters={"SDate": "2005-01-01", "EDate": "2024-12-31",  "Frq": "M", "period":"FY1"}) 
rd.close_session() 

# Print 
print(eps_fy1)

# Save both tables to CSV
eps_fy1.to_csv("msft_eps_fy1_monthly.csv")

    Instrument  Earnings Per Share - Mean       Date  \
0       MSFT.O                    1.28345 2005-01-31   
1       MSFT.O                    1.28357 2005-02-14   
2       MSFT.O                    1.28536 2005-03-31   
3       MSFT.O                    1.29321 2005-04-29   
4       MSFT.O                    1.29419 2005-05-09   
..         ...                        ...        ...   
235     MSFT.O                   13.06888 2024-08-27   
236     MSFT.O                   13.06876 2024-09-25   
237     MSFT.O                   13.01446 2024-10-31   
238     MSFT.O                    13.0223 2024-11-27   
239     MSFT.O                    13.0048 2024-12-22   

     Earnings Per Share - Actual  
0                            1.3  
1                            1.3  
2                            1.3  
3                            1.3  
4                            1.3  
..                           ...  
235                        13.64  
236                        13.64  
237                        13.64  
238                        13.64  
239                        13.64  

[240 rows x 4 columns]
In [40]:
import refinitiv.data as rd 
rd.open_session()
# period 'FY2' (in two fiscal year)
eps_fy2 = rd.get_data(
    universe="MSFT.O",  
    fields=[ "TR.EPSMean",  "TR.EPSMean.Date",    "TR.EPSActValue" ],  
    parameters={"SDate": "2005-01-01", "EDate": "2024-12-31",  "Frq": "M", "period":"FY2"}) 
rd.close_session() 

# Print 
print(eps_fy2)

# Save both tables to CSV
eps_fy2.to_csv("msft_eps_fy2_monthly.csv")

1       MSFT.O                    1.40412 2005-02-14   
2       MSFT.O                    1.40059 2005-03-31   
3       MSFT.O                    1.36571 2005-04-29   
4       MSFT.O                    1.34833 2005-05-20   
..         ...                        ...        ...

236     MSFT.O                    15.2152 2024-09-25   
237     MSFT.O                   15.00293 2024-10-31   
238     MSFT.O                   14.96288 2024-11-27   
239     MSFT.O                   14.96963 2024-12-22   

     Earnings Per Share - Actual  
0                           1.27  
1                           1.27  
2                           1.27  
3                           1.27  
4                           1.27  
..                           ...

2.10. Extracting FX and crypto time series¶

In this subsection we show how to extract foreign exchange (FX) and cryptocurrency time series. The pattern is the same as before: we use rd.get_history(), specify the instrument RIC, choose the frequency, and select the fields.

For FX, we use standard currency RICs (for example "EUR=" for the EUR/USD exchange rate). For cryptocurrencies, you must first look up the correct RIC in Refinitiv Workspace or DIB (for example by searching for "Bitcoin").

We request only the BID (price a buyer offers) and ASK (price a seller accepts) fields and then compute the bid-ask spread (= ASK − BID) in Python. The spread measures the transaction cost of trading: a smaller spread means a more liquid instrument. The next cell retrieves daily (1-minute) FX data for EUR/USD (bid and ask), and generate spread.

In [41]:
import refinitiv.data as rd  # Refinitiv/LSEG Data Library (requires credentials)
rd.open_session()
# ── Daily BID and ASK for a full calendar year fo FX (EURUSD) ────────────────────────────────
fx_daily = rd.get_history(  
    universe="EUR=",  
    fields=["BID", "ASK"],  
    interval="daily",  
    start="2024-01-01",
    end="2024-12-31") 
rd.close_session() 
# Compute the bid-ask spread = ASK − BID.
fx_daily["SPREAD"] = fx_daily["ASK"] - fx_daily["BID"] 
#Prints
print(fx_daily.head())
# Save to CSV.
fx_daily.to_csv("eur_fx_daily_2024.csv")

EUR=           BID     ASK  SPREAD
Date                              
2024-01-02  1.0947  1.0951  0.0004
2024-01-03  1.0921  1.0922  0.0001
2024-01-04  1.0943  1.0947  0.0004
2024-01-05  1.0941  1.0943  0.0002
2024-01-08  1.0949  1.0953  0.0004

The next cell repeats the same pattern for a cryptocurrency (Bitcoin, "BTC="). Replace the RIC with the correct one from Refinitiv Workspace if needed.

In [42]:
import refinitiv.data as rd
rd.open_session()
# Download daily BID and ASK for Bitcoin over 2024.
crypto_daily = rd.get_history( 
    universe= "BTC=", 
    fields=["BID", "ASK"],  
    interval="daily", 
    start="2024-01-01",  
    end="2024-12-31") 
rd.close_session()
# Compute the bid-ask spread.
crypto_daily["SPREAD"] = crypto_daily["ASK"] - crypto_daily["BID"] 
# print
print(crypto_daily.head())
# save
crypto_daily.to_csv("crypto_daily_2024.csv")

BTC=             BID       ASK  SPREAD
Date                                  
2024-01-02   45124.0   45125.0     1.0
2024-01-03   42939.0   42949.0    10.0
2024-01-04   44488.0   44490.0     2.0
2024-01-05  43960.11  43961.74    1.63
2024-01-06   43919.0   43920.0     1.0

2.11. Extracting ESG information for companies¶

ESG stands for Environmental, Social, and Governance — three broad categories used to evaluate a company's behaviour beyond pure financial performance. Refinitiv provides ESG scores that summarise how well a company performs on each dimension.

Key fields:

Field Description
TR.TRESGScore Overall ESG score (0–100) based on reported data
TR.TRESGCScore Combined ESG score, discounted for significant controversies
TR.EnvironmentPillarScore Environmental pillar score
TR.SocialPillarScore Social pillar score
TR.GovernancePillarScore Governance pillar score
TR.ControvPrivacy Example controversy metric (privacy-related)

The cell below retrieves a 10-year ESG history and a controversy metric for Microsoft (MSFT.O). The same pattern works for any RIC or list of RICs.

In [43]:
import refinitiv.data as rd
rd.open_session()
esg_history = rd.get_data(
    universe="MSFT.O",
    fields=[
        "TR.TRESGScore",              # overall ESG score
        "TR.TRESGScore.date",         # associated date
        "TR.TRESGCScore",             # combined (controversy-adjusted) score
        "TR.EnvironmentPillarScore",  # E pillar score
        "TR.SocialPillarScore",  # S pillar score
        "TR.GovernancePillarScore", # G pillar score
    ], 
    parameters={"SDate": "2015-01-01", "EDate": "2024-12-31", "Frq": "FY"})
rd.close_session()
# print
print(esg_history)
# save
esg_history.to_csv("esg_history_MSFT.csv")

  Instrument  ESG Score       Date  ESG Combined Score  \
0     MSFT.O  92.388173 2014-06-30           50.360753   
1     MSFT.O  92.837502 2015-06-30           61.636142

In [44]:
import refinitiv.data as rd
rd.open_session()
controversy_history = rd.get_data(
    universe="MSFT.O",
    fields=["TR.ControvPrivacy", "TR.ControvPrivacy.Date"], #an ESG controversy metric, 
    parameters={"SDate": "2015-01-01", "EDate": "2024-12-31", "Frq": "FY"})
rd.close_session()
# print
print(controversy_history)
# save
controversy_history.to_csv("controversy_history_MSFT.csv")

  Instrument  Controversies Privacy       Date
0     MSFT.O                      3 2014-06-30
1     MSFT.O                      1 2015-06-30
2     MSFT.O                      4 2016-06-30
3     MSFT.O                      1 2017-06-30
4     MSFT.O                      6 2018-06-30
5     MSFT.O                      2 2019-06-30

9     MSFT.O                      4 2023-06-30

Application. Now it is your turn to practice. For all the constituent of the CAC40, collect the time series of yearly total carbon emission (scope 1, 2, 3) over the last five years. First, search for the right variable name in Refinitiv. A solution is provided below (click to unhide it).

In [45]:
import pandas as pd 
import refinitiv.data as rd 
rd.open_session()
# Get constituent
constituents = pd.read_csv("cac40_constituents.csv") 
rics = constituents["RIC"].tolist()
co2_history = rd.get_data( 
    universe=rics, 
    fields=["TR.AnalyticEstimatedCO2Total", "TR.AnalyticEstimatedCO2Total.Date"],  
    parameters={"SDate": "2020-01-01", "EDate": "2024-12-31", "Frq": "FY"})
rd.close_session()
# Print
print(co2_history)
# Save to CSV.
co2_history.to_csv("cac40_co2.csv")

    Instrument  Estimated CO2 Equivalents Emission Total       Date
0      BNPP.PA                                    282872 2020-12-31
1      BNPP.PA                                    251617 2021-12-31
2      BNPP.PA                                    242639 2022-12-31
3      BNPP.PA                                    162724 2023-12-31
4      BNPP.PA                                    152923 2024-12-31
..         ...                                       ...        ...
194     BVI.PA                                    142401 2020-12-31
195     BVI.PA                                    164493 2021-12-31
196     BVI.PA                                    158774 2022-12-31
197     BVI.PA                                    158639 2023-12-31
198     BVI.PA                                    158704 2024-12-31

[199 rows x 3 columns]

2.12. Extracting fund information¶

Refinitiv provides detailed data on mutual funds and ETFs (Exchange-Traded Funds), including fund identification, investment objectives, performance over various horizons, risk metrics, and portfolio holdings. The exact fields available depend on your institution's subscription.

We search for funds using the fund screener or type the name of find in the Refinitiv search bar.

The cell below retrieves identification, performance, risk, and holdings data for an example fund (Mirova Global Sustainability Fund, Lipper ID LP68603610). We also request a 10-year rolling monthly performance series.

For full documentation on rd.get_data() parameters, see the official LSEG reference or call help(rd.get_data).

In [46]:
import refinitiv.data as rd
rd.open_session()
# Retrieve metadata for the fund view.
meta = rd.content.search.metadata.Definition( 
    view=rd.content.search.Views.FUND_QUOTES
).get_data() 
print(meta.data.df[["Type", "Searchable", "Exact"]].to_string())
rd.close_session()

How do I know possible values for one variable (or more) from one view to then put in place a screen/filter? I can use a navigator. Navigators provide the ability to summarize the distribution of your results. They are particularly useful when you are interested in gathering the domain of values for a specific property. or simply to understand how to build your filters based on specific values contained within Properties. Navigators can be used against a specific View, used in conjunction with either a query, a filter, or both. Navigators can be simple or very powerful, but provide a very useful way to capture results in logical buckets.

In [47]:
import refinitiv.data as rd
rd.open_session()
response = rd.content.search.Definition(
    top=0,
    navigators="AssetCategoryName").get_data() 
rd.close_session() 
response.data.raw['Navigators']['AssetCategoryName']

In [48]:
# We now know what we can use in the filters
import refinitiv.data as rd
rd.open_session()
# Search for fund called mirova something
fund_list = rd.discovery.search(
    view=rd.discovery.Views.FUND_QUOTES,
    query="Mirova",
    top=100) 
#show list
fund_list.head(10)

In [49]:
# For one fund, show performance metrics
import refinitiv.data as rd 
rd.open_session()
# Example fund: Mirova Global Green Bond Fund 
fund_universe = ["LP68234916"] 
# Variable we are interested in
fund_fields = [ 
    "TR.FundName",              # fund name
    "TR.FundObjective",         # stated investment objective
    "TR.FundType",              # fund type (equity, bond, mixed, etc.)
    "TR.FundGeographicFocus",   # geographic focus
    "TR.FundLaunchDate",        # date the fund was launched
    "TR.FundTER",               # Total Expense Ratio
    "TR.FundAlpha1Year",        # Jensen's alpha over 1 year
    "TR.FundAlpha3Year", 
    "TR.FundAlpha5Year", 
    "TR.FundRelPerf6Mth",       # relative performance vs benchmark
    "TR.FundRelPerf1Year", 
    "TR.FundRelPerf3Year", 
    "TR.FundRelPerf5Year", 
    "TR.FundRelPerf10Year", 
    "TR.FundStdDev1Year",       # standard deviation (volatility) over 1 year
    "TR.FundStdDev3Year", 
    "TR.FundStdDev5Year", 
    "TR.FundStdDev10Year", 
] 
# Collect data
fund_info = rd.get_data( 
    universe=fund_universe,
    fields=fund_fields) 
rd.close_session() 
# Print
print(fund_info.T)   # transpose so each field appears as a row
# Save
fund_info.to_csv("fund_information_example.csv")

In [50]:
# Get fund holdings
import refinitiv.data as rd
rd.open_session() 
# Example fund: Mirova Global Sustainability Fund (Lipper ID).
fund_universe = ["LP68603610"]
# collect holdings
fund_holdings = rd.get_data(  
    universe=fund_universe,
    fields=[
        "TR.FundHoldingRIC", 
        "TR.FundHoldingName", 
        "TR.FundNumberOfShares", 
        "TR.FundPercentageOfFundAssets" 
    ])
rd.close_session() 
# print first holdings
print(fund_holdings.head(10)) 
# save portfolio
fund_holdings.to_csv("fund_holdings.csv")

In [51]:
# Get rolling perf
import refinitiv.data as rd 
rd.open_session() 
# Example fund: Mirova Global Sustainability Fund (Lipper ID).
fund_universe = ["LP68603610"]
fund_perf = rd.get_history(  
    universe=fund_universe[0], 
    fields=["TR.FundRollingPerformance(RollTimeFrame=10Y,Interval=M)"])
rd.close_session()
print(fund_perf.head(10)) 
fund_perf.to_csv("fund_monthly_performance.csv")

2.13. Extracting M&A deal information¶

Refinitiv maintains a comprehensive Mergers & Acquisitions (M&A) deals database. We can search and filter deals using the DEALS_MERGERS_AND_ACQUISITIONS Search view.

Before building a query, it is useful to inspect the metadata of the view to see all available properties, their types, and whether they can be used as filters. The first cell below does this. The second cell then runs an actual deal search.

For detailed Python examples, see the LSEG search tutorials: Search with the Data Library.

In [52]:
import refinitiv.data as rd
rd.open_session()
# Retrieve metadata for the Deals M&A view.
meta = rd.content.search.metadata.Definition(
    view=rd.content.search.Views.DEALS_MERGERS_AND_ACQUISITIONS 
).get_data()
rd.close_session() 
# print
print(meta.data.df[["Type", "Searchable", "Exact"]].to_string())

How do I know possible values for one variable (or more) from one view to then put in place a screen/filter? I can use a navigator. Navigators provide the ability to summarize the distribution of your results. They are particularly useful when you are interested in gathering the domain of values for a specific property. or simply to understand how to build your filters based on specific values contained within Properties. Navigators can be used against a specific View, used in conjunction with either a query, a filter, or both. Navigators can be simple or very powerful, but provide a very useful way to capture results in logical buckets.

In [53]:
import refinitiv.data as rd  
rd.open_session()
# one example 
response = rd.content.search.Definition(  
    top=0,  
    navigators="TransactionStatus"
).get_data() 
rd.close_session()
# show possible values for this variable
response.data.raw['Navigators']['TransactionStatus']
Out[53]:
{'Buckets': [{'Label': 'Completed', 'Count': 1245007},
  {'Label': 'Pending', 'Count': 183495},

  {'Label': 'Intended', 'Count': 36081},
  {'Label': 'Status Unknown', 'Count': 35762},

  {'Label': 'Seeking Buyer Withdrawn', 'Count': 2613},
  {'Label': 'Intent Withdrawn', 'Count': 1034},
  {'Label': 'Rumor', 'Count': 780},
  {'Label': 'Unconditional', 'Count': 473},
  {'Label': 'Seeking Target', 'Count': 259},
  {'Label': 'Pending Regulatory', 'Count': 22},
  {'Label': 'Partially Completed', 'Count': 16}]}

import refinitiv.data as rd
rd.open_session()  
# anotehr one: Target Public Status
response = rd.content.search.Definition(  
    top=0, 
    navigators="TargetPublicStatus"
).get_data() 
rd.close_session()
response.data.raw['Navigators']['TargetPublicStatus']
Out[ ]:
{'Buckets': [{'Label': 'Private', 'Count': 771322},
  {'Label': 'Subsidiary', 'Count': 535982},

The cell below searches for completed M&A deals where the target is a French private company, the deal value (including net debt) is at least 50 million, the form of the transaction is a merger or acquisition, and the announcement date falls between 2020 and 2025. Results are sorted by announcement date (most recent first).

A complete guide on how to set up filters and use the VIEWS in Refinitiv with Python can be found here.

In [55]:
#now that we now the filter valuse we can use, we set up a filter 
import refinitiv.data as rd 
rd.open_session()
# Search for French private-target M&A deals, completed, value >= 50M, 2020–2025.
MnA = rd.discovery.search(  
    view=rd.discovery.Views.DEALS_MERGERS_AND_ACQUISITIONS,
    filter=(
        "(TargetCountry eq 'FR' " 
        "and TransactionValueIncludingNetDebtOfTarget ge 50 " 
        "and TargetPublicStatus eq 'Private' " 
        "and TransactionStatus eq 'Completed' " 
        "and (FormOfTransactionName xeq 'Merger' " 
        "     or FormOfTransactionName xeq 'Acquisition') " 
        "and TransactionAnnouncementDate le 2025-12-31 " 
        "and TransactionAnnouncementDate ge 2020-01-01)" 
    ), 
    select=(
        "TransactionAnnouncementDate, TargetCompanyName, TargetRIC, " 
        "TargetCountry, TargetPublicStatus, AcquirerCompanyName, " 
        "TransactionStatus, TransactionValueIncludingNetDebtOfTarget, " 
        "FormOfTransactionName" 
    ), 
    order_by="TransactionAnnouncementDate desc",
    top=1000
) 

# Save to CSV and display the first rows.
MnA.to_csv("ma_deals_FR.csv")
MnA.head(10)
Out[55]:
TransactionAnnouncementDate TargetCompanyName TargetCountry TargetPublicStatus AcquirerCompanyName TransactionStatus TransactionValueIncludingNetDebtOfTarget FormOfTransactionName
0 2025-10-22 ImCheck Therapeutics SAS FR Private Ipsen SA Completed 405.93 Merger
1 2025-02-20 Asc3 Lmb Topco Sas FR Private Loar Group Inc Completed 380.366 Merger
2 2025-02-13 Stilla Technologies SAS FR Private Bio-Rad Laboratories Inc Completed 274.998 Merger
3 2024-10-07 Younited SA FR Private RA Special Acquisition Corp Completed 219.52 Merger
4 2024-09-25 Financiere Hmy Sas FR Private Itab Shop Concept AB Completed 357.76 Merger
5 2024-06-24 Preligens SAS FR Private Safran SA Completed 236.564 Merger
6 2023-06-16 Arverne Group SAS FR Private Transition SA Completed 181.687 Merger
7 2023-05-05 Wargraphs SAS FR Private MOBA Network AB Completed 55.055 Merger
8 2022-11-29 Cenexi SAS FR Private Gland Pharma International Pte Ltd Completed 125.935 Merger
9 2022-07-28 ATALIAN International SAS FR Private Clayton Dubilier & Rice LLC Completed 768.211 Merger

2.14. Extracting private equity deal information¶

Refinitiv also maintains a Private Equity and Venture Capital deals database. Access is via the INVESTORS Search view and the M&A deals view. The exact field names and filter values are documented in the Private Equity data guide and can be explored via DIB.

Navigators are a useful feature of the LSEG Search API: they return the distinct categorical values for a given field (similar to a "group by" in SQL). This lets you discover, for example, all possible values of InvestorType or InvestorAddressCountry before writing a filter.

The first cell below retrieves navigator buckets for the INVESTORS view. The second cell searches for French private equity investors. The third cell retrieves M&A deals for a specific PE firm.

In [56]:
import refinitiv.data as rd
rd.open_session()
# Retrieve metadata for the INVESTORS view.
meta = rd.content.search.metadata.Definition(  
    view=rd.content.search.Views.INVESTORS
).get_data() 
print(meta.data.df[["Type", "Searchable", "Exact"]].to_string())
rd.close_session() 

                              Type  Searchable  Exact
BusinessEntity              String        True   True
DocumentTitle               String        True  False
DTCharacteristics           String       False  False
DTSimpleType                String        True   True
DTSubjectName               String        True  False
HKAlias                     String        True   True
HKVersion                   String        True  False
InvestorAddressCity         String        True   True
InvestorAddressCountry      String        True   True
InvestorContactName         String        True  False
InvestorContactPermID       String        True   True
InvestorEntityType          String        True   True
InvestorEquityAssets        Double        True  False
InvestorFundParentName      String        True   True
InvestorFundParentOAPermID  String        True   True
InvestorId                  String        True  False
InvestorInvestmentStyle     String        True   True
InvestorLatestFilingDate      Date        True  False
InvestorName                String        True   True
InvestorOrientation         String        True   True
InvestorSubType             String        True   True
InvestorTurnover            String        True   True
InvestorType                String        True   True
PermID                      String        True   True
PI                          String        True  False
RIC                         String        True   True
SearchAllCategoryv3         String        True   True
Source                      String        True  False
SubjectBoost                String        True  False

The next cell uses navigators to see the distinct values for InvestorType, InvestorSubType, InvestorEntityType, and InvestorAddressCountry. Setting top=100 returns 100 document hits alongside the navigator buckets.

In [57]:
import refinitiv.data as rd 
rd.open_session()
# navigator to get all the possible values for InvestorSubType
response = rd.content.search.Definition(
    top=0,  
    navigators="InvestorSubType"
).get_data() 
rd.close_session() 
response.data.raw['Navigators']['InvestorSubType']

The next cell searches for all French private equity investors, and the one after retrieves M&A deals for a specific PE firm.

In [59]:
import refinitiv.data as rd  
rd.open_session()
french_pe_investors = rd.discovery.search(
    view=rd.discovery.Views.INVESTORS,  
    filter="InvestorSubType eq 'Private Equity' and InvestorAddressCountry eq 'France'",  
    select="InvestorName, InvestorSubType",
    top=200) 
rd.close_session() 
print(french_pe_investors.to_string())

                                                   InvestorName InvestorSubType
0                                                  Salvepar_NLE  Private Equity
1                                  Développement et partenariat  Private Equity
2                        Initiative & Finance Investissement SA  Private Equity
3                               Amundi Private Equity Funds SAS  Private Equity
4                                              NextStage AM SAS  Private Equity
5                                              Naxicap Partners  Private Equity
6                                    BNP Paribas Private Equity  Private Equity
7                                 NBGI Private Equity S.A.S_NLE  Private Equity
8                                     LBO France Gestion S.A.S.  Private Equity

The next cell retrieves M&A deals for one specific PE firm (BNP Paribas Private Equity).

In [60]:
import refinitiv.data as rd 
import pandas as pd
rd.open_session()
fund_name = "BNP Paribas Private Equity"
deals = rd.discovery.search(
    view=rd.discovery.Views.DEALS_MERGERS_AND_ACQUISITIONS,
    filter=f"AcquirerCompanyName eq '{fund_name}'",
    select=("TransactionAnnouncementDate, AcquirerCompanyName,TargetCompanyName"),
    order_by="TransactionAnnouncementDate desc",
    top=500) 
print(deals.to_string())

  TransactionAnnouncementDate                           AcquirerCompanyName         TargetCompanyName
0                  2022-02-10  BNP Paribas Fortis Private Equity Belgium NV              Hannecard NV
1                  2021-07-23  BNP Paribas Fortis Private Equity Belgium NV                Konings NV
2                  2018-01-01  BNP Paribas Fortis Private Equity Belgium NV  Food Associates Group NV
3                  2010-06-21                BNP Paribas Private Equity SAS      Acropolis Telecom SA
4                  2010-05-31                BNP Paribas Private Equity SAS                Telogic AS
5                  2006-03-31                BNP Paribas Private Equity SAS              AgendiZe SAS

2.15. Extracting director and CEO information¶

Refinitiv provides officer and director data for listed companies: names, titles, ages, gender, and biographies. The workflow is:

  1. Search for the company's equity RIC using the EQUITY_QUOTES view.
  2. Use rd.get_data() with officer fields (e.g. TR.OfficerName, TR.OfficerTitle, TR.OfficerAge, TR.ODOfficerPersonGender, TR.ODOfficerBiography).
  3. Control how many officers are returned via ranking parameters (Rnk for officer fields, ODRnk for OD-prefixed fields).

The example below retrieves board and management information for Dassault.

In [61]:
import refinitiv.data as rd 
rd.open_session() 

# ── STEP 1: Find Dassault equity RIC ─────────────────────────────────────────
equity_hits = rd.discovery.search( 
    query="Dassault",  
    view=rd.discovery.Views.EQUITY_QUOTES, 
    top=20) 

# Take the first candidate (replace with the specific RIC you want).
selected_ric = equity_hits.iloc[0]["RIC"]
print("Selected RIC:", {selected_ric})

# ── STEP 2: Retrieve officer / director details ──────────────────────────────
officers = rd.get_data( 
    universe=[selected_ric], 
    fields=["TR.OfficerName", 
        "TR.OfficerTitle", 
        "TR.OfficerAge", 
        "TR.ODOfficerPersonGender"], 
    parameters={"Rnk": "R1:R20", "ODRnk": "R1:R20"}) 
rd.close_session() 
# Print
print(officers)
# Save
officers.to_csv("dassault_officers_raw.csv")

Selected RIC: {'DAST.PA'}

2.16. Extracting news flow for a company¶

Refinitiv provides access to news headlines and full stories for listed companies. News data can be used to study information arrival, sentiment, or to link specific events to stock price reactions.

The workflow has two steps:

  1. rd.news.get_headlines() — Retrieves a table of headlines for a given RIC and date range. The query parameter uses Refinitiv query syntax: "R:NVDA.O" targets the NVIDIA RIC, and "Language:LEN" restricts to English-language news.
  2. rd.news.get_story() — Retrieves the full text of a specific news story, identified by its storyId from the headlines table.

For full documentation, call help(rd.news.get_headlines) and help(rd.news.get_story), or see the official LSEG reference.

In [62]:
import refinitiv.data as rd 
# Display documentation for the headline retrieval function.
help(rd.news.get_headlines) 

Help on function get_headlines in module refinitiv.data._access_layer.news._news:

get_headlines(query: str, count: int = 10, start: 'OptDateTime' = None, end: 'OptDateTime' = None, order_by: Union[str, refinitiv.data.content.news.headlines._sort_order.SortOrder] = <SortOrder.new_to_old: 'newToOld'>) -> pandas.core.frame.DataFrame
    Retrieves news headlines.

    Parameters
    ----------
    query: str
        The user search query for news headlines.
    count: int, optional
        Count to limit number of headlines.
    start: str or timedelta, optional
        Beginning of date range.
        String format is: '%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S'. e.g. '2016-01-20T15:04:05'.
    end: str or timedelta, optional
        End of date range.
        String format is: '%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S'. e.g. '2016-01-20T15:04:05'.
    order_by: str or SortOrder
        Sort order for headline items.

    Returns
    -------
    pd.DataFrame
        Headlines dataframe

    Examples
    --------
    >>> from datetime import timedelta
    >>> import refinitiv.data as rd
    >>> response = rd.news.get_headlines(
    ...     "Refinitiv",
    ...     start="20.03.2021",
    ...     end=timedelta(days=-4),
    ...     count=3
    ... )
In [63]:
#use the function to get headlines for a company: for NVIDIA over 2024
import refinitiv.data as rd
rd.open_session()
ric = "NVDA.O"   # NVIDIA

# ── STEP 1: Get headlines ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# "R:{ric}" targets the specific instrument; "Language:LEN" = English only.
headlines = rd.news.get_headlines(  
    query=f"R:{ric} AND Language:LEN", 
    start="2024-01-01",  
    end="2024-12-31",
    count=100               # maximum headlines per call
) 
# Print
print(headlines)

[100 rows x 3 columns]

The next cell loops through each headline retrieved above, fetches the full story text using rd.news.get_story(), and saves everything to an Excel file. We add a 0.2-second pause between calls to avoid hitting the Refinitiv API rate limit. This cell depends on the headlines DataFrame created just above.

Before retrieving full story text, let us inspect the documentation of rd.news.get_story().

In [64]:
import refinitiv.data as rd 
# Display documentation for the story retrieval function.
help(rd.news.get_story) 

Help on function get_story in module refinitiv.data._access_layer.news._news:

get_story(story_id: str, format: Union[refinitiv.data._access_layer.news._news.Format, str] = <Format.HTML: 'Html'>) -> str
    Retrieves the news story items.

    Parameters
    ----------
    story_id : str
        News Story ID.
    format : str, Format, optional
        Response format.

    Returns
    -------
    str
        Story html or text response

    Examples
    --------
    >>> import refinitiv.data as rd
    >>> response = rd.news.get_story("urn:newsml:reuters.com:20220713:nL1N2YU10J", format=rd.news.Format.TEXT)
In [69]:
import refinitiv.data as rd 
import time
rd.open_session() 

# ── STEP 2: Get full story text for each headline ────────────────────────────
# This cell depends on the 'headlines' DataFrame from the cell above.
# Take times to run, refer to xlsx file!
results = []  # variable assignment: store a value in 'results' so we can reuse it later
for _, row in headlines.iterrows():  # for-loop: repeat code for each element in an iterable
    # Retrieve the full text of each story by its storyId.
    story_text = rd.news.get_story(row["storyId"], format=rd.news.Format.TEXT) 
    results.append({ 
        "headline": row["headline"], 
        "storyId":  row["storyId"], 
        "text":     story_text 
    }) 
    time.sleep(0.2)   # pause 0.2 seconds between calls to avoid rate limiting

df_news = pd.DataFrame(results) 
print(df_news[["headline"]].to_string())  # print() outputs

# Save to Excel
df_news.to_excel('news_NVDA.xlsx')

# Close Session
rd.close_session()
